refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is conditional on 'agreed network conditions' (a boundary condition) and ties acceptance to a measurable, customer-negotiated metric rather than a fixed value.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct end-to-end packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth constraints) agreed with customer. Measure across multiple teleoperation sessions and load profiles. Acceptance: sustained packet loss ≤1% (or customer-defined target) over a minimum test duration (e.g., 1 hour continuous operation).
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation subsystem shall implement packet loss detection and reporting.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation subsystem shall employ error correction or retransmission mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact on command/feedback integrity.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation subsystem shall adapt communication parameters (e.g., compression, redundancy) based on network conditions to maintain packet loss within acceptable bounds.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The safety services subsystem shall monitor teleoperation communication quality and trigger safe-state transitions if packet loss exceeds threshold.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement adaptive bitrate encoding and forward error correction (FEC) in the teleoperation link, with real-time packet loss monitoring and automatic fallback to reduced-fidelity mode if threshold is exceeded.
DP-B: Deploy redundant communication channels (primary + backup network paths) with automatic failover and packet aggregation to mask individual packet loss events.

DP-C: Implement application-level acknowledgment and selective retransmission of critical command/feedback packets, combined with jitter buffering and predictive interpolation to mask transient packet loss.
DP-D: Hybrid approach combining FEC (DP-A), redundant channels (DP-B), and selective retransmission (DP-C) with dynamic mode selection based on real-time network quality metrics.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Hybrid approach combining forward error correction (FEC) for real-time telemetry, redundant communication channels with automatic failover for critical commands, and application-level selective retransmission with jitter buffering. Network quality monitoring triggers dynamic mode selection (high-fidelity vs. reduced-fidelity) to maintain packet loss ≤1% under agreed conditions.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation subsystem design to network infrastructure assumptions (availability of dual paths, bandwidth for redundancy). Mitigation: define network SLA requirements explicitly.
DP-D couples safety services to teleoperation quality monitoring. Mitigation: define clear thresholds and safe-state transition logic in safety services design.
DP-D introduces complexity in mode-switching logic; potential for race conditions or delayed transitions. Mitigation: rigorous state machine design and testing.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is preferred over single-mechanism approaches (A, B, C) because it provides defense-in-depth: FEC handles random bit errors, redundancy handles transient path failures, and retransmission handles isolated packet drops. Hybrid approach reduces dependency on any single mechanism and improves robustness across diverse network conditions. Coupling is manageable with clear interface contracts and safety monitoring.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer-agreed network conditions are well-defined and stable (e.g., 4G LTE, WiFi 6, or private network with specified latency/jitter bounds). Risk: If conditions are vague or highly variable, packet loss target may be unachievable. Mitigation: Establish network SLA with customer before design finalization.

Assumption: Redundant communication channels are available (e.g., dual cellular, WiFi + LTE, or dual fiber). Risk: If only single network path exists, DP-D redundancy component cannot be realized. Mitigation: Confirm network topology with customer and site infrastructure team.

Assumption: FEC overhead (bandwidth and latency) is acceptable for teleoperation use case. Risk: Excessive FEC overhead may increase latency beyond operator tolerance. Mitigation: Prototype and measure latency impact; tune FEC parameters.

Assumption: Safety services can reliably detect and respond to packet loss threshold violations. Risk: Delayed detection or false positives could compromise safety. Mitigation: Implement deterministic monitoring with clear thresholds and test safe-state transitions.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) FEC scheme (e.g., Reed-Solomon, Turbo codes) and overhead budget; (2) redundancy strategy (e.g., active-active vs. active-passive failover); (3) retransmission timeout and retry limits; (4) network quality metrics and thresholds for mode switching; (5) safe-state transition logic and latency budget. These details will inform IM assignment and safety validation.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end communication pipeline for remote equipment operation, including command transmission, telemetry reception, packet loss detection, FEC encoding/decoding, redundant channel management, selective retransmission, and mode adaptation. Interfaces with Vehicle (for command actuation and sensor data), Safety Services (for quality monitoring and safe-state triggers), and Network Link (for physical transmission).

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands (movement, tool control, mode selection) from tele-operator console or autonomous planner. Teleoperation module encodes and transmits via Network Link with FEC and redundancy.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, equipment status) to tele-operator console and safety services. Teleoperation module applies FEC, redundancy, and jitter buffering to ensure fidelity despite packet loss.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives packet loss threshold violations from Safety Services; transmits communication quality metrics (packet loss %, latency, jitter) to Safety Services for monitoring. Triggers safe-state transitions if thresholds exceeded.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Physical/logical communication channels (primary and backup) used by Teleoperation module to transmit/receive packets. Teleoperation abstracts redundancy and failover from Network Link layer.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Teleoperation is a major subsystem but not the entire solution. Assigning DP-D to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility across multiple sub-teams and obscure ownership of packet loss accountability. Not recommended as primary IM boundary.

Vehicle module owns physical equipment (motors, sensors, hydraulics). Teleoperation communication logic is orthogonal to vehicle mechanics. Assigning DP-D to Vehicle would create tight coupling between vehicle control and network communication, violating separation of concerns. Not recommended.

Teleoperation module is the natural home for DP-D. It owns command/feedback encoding, FEC, redundancy management, retransmission logic, and mode adaptation. All sub-components of DP-D (FEC encoder/decoder, redundancy controller, retransmission buffer, quality monitor) are cohesive and belong in this module. Clear ownership and minimal diffusion.

Safety Services owns monitoring and safe-state transitions, not the communication mechanisms themselves. Assigning DP-D to Safety Services would conflate communication implementation with safety logic. Safety Services should consume packet loss metrics from Teleoperation and trigger responses, not implement the communication layer. Not recommended as primary IM.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal IM boundary for DP-D because: (1) it owns the full communication pipeline (encoding, transmission, reception, decoding); (2) FEC, redundancy, and retransmission are communication-layer concerns, not vehicle or safety concerns; (3) it provides a clear, cohesive ownership boundary with minimal diffusion; (4) it interfaces cleanly with Vehicle (via Tasking/Telemetry), Safety Services (via SafetyAlert), and Network Link (via physical channels); (5) it enables independent testing and optimization of packet loss mitigation without coupling to vehicle or safety subsystems.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become complex if FEC, redundancy, and retransmission logic are not well-modularized internally. Mitigation: Decompose Teleoperation into sub-components (FEC codec, redundancy controller, retransmission buffer, quality monitor) with clear internal interfaces.

Risk: Teleoperation module depends on Safety Services for threshold monitoring and safe-state triggers. If Safety Services is slow to respond, packet loss may exceed threshold before mitigation. Mitigation: Define deterministic monitoring latency budget and test integration.

Risk: Teleoperation module assumes Network Link provides dual channels. If network topology changes, DP-D redundancy component may not be realizable. Mitigation: Establish network SLA and document assumptions in interface contracts.

Risk: Mode switching (high-fidelity vs. reduced-fidelity) may introduce operator confusion or safety issues if not clearly communicated. Mitigation: Ensure SafetyAlert interface clearly signals mode changes to operator and safety services.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine Teleoperation IM boundary to explicitly define: (1) internal sub-components (FEC codec, redundancy controller, retransmission buffer, quality monitor) and their interfaces; (2) dependencies on Network Link (dual channels, bandwidth, latency SLA); (3) dependencies on Safety Services (threshold definitions, safe-state transition latency); (4) mode switching logic and operator notification; (5) testing strategy for packet loss scenarios (simulation, testbed, field trials). These details will support detailed design and integration planning.

fr_dp_gate.reason: Refined FR-NGHE-009 clearly states the requirement (packet loss ≤1% under agreed conditions) and is measurable. Selected DP-D (hybrid FEC + redundancy + retransmission) directly addresses the requirement through multiple complementary mechanisms. DP-D is technically sound and feasible. No fundamental mismatch between FR and DP.

diffusion_risks: DP-D couples Teleoperation to Safety Services for quality monitoring. Mitigation: Define clear SafetyAlert interface contract with latency and threshold specifications.
DP-D couples Teleoperation to Network Link for redundant channels. Mitigation: Define Network Link SLA (dual paths, bandwidth, latency) in interface contract.
DP-D introduces mode switching logic that may affect operator experience. Mitigation: Ensure clear communication of mode changes via SafetyAlert and operator console.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module is the optimal IM boundary for DP-D. Containment score 0.95 indicates strong alignment. FEC, redundancy, and retransmission are communication-layer concerns that belong in Teleoperation, not in Vehicle, Safety Services, or NGHE Solution. Clear ownership and minimal diffusion across module boundaries.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-009 is feasible via end-to-end packet loss measurement under agreed network conditions. Teleoperation module can expose packet loss metrics (via SafetyAlert interface) for monitoring and acceptance testing. Testbed and field trials can validate DP-D effectiveness.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of FR-NGHE-009 to Teleoperation IM is clear. Teleoperation owns packet loss mitigation mechanisms (FEC, redundancy, retransmission). Safety Services owns monitoring and safe-state transitions. Vehicle owns command actuation and sensor data. Network Link owns physical transmission. Clear separation of concerns.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D to specify FEC scheme, redundancy strategy, retransmission parameters, and mode switching thresholds. These details are needed for detailed design and integration.
Establish network SLA with customer (dual channels, bandwidth, latency, jitter bounds) before finalizing DP-D. Confirm that agreed network conditions support ≤1% packet loss target.
Define SafetyAlert interface contract: packet loss metrics (%, latency, jitter), threshold definitions, safe-state transition logic, and latency budget for detection and response.
Decompose Teleoperation IM into sub-components (FEC codec, redundancy controller, retransmission buffer, quality monitor) with clear internal interfaces to manage complexity.

Plan testing strategy: unit tests for FEC/redundancy/retransmission logic, integration tests with Safety Services and Network Link, testbed validation under simulated packet loss, field trials under real network conditions.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-009 is not atomic and has been decomposed into four sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-009.1 through 009.4) addressing packet loss detection, error correction, adaptation, and safety monitoring. DP-D is sound and well-justified. Teleoperation IM is the optimal boundary with high containment (0.95) and low diffusion (0.05). All gates pass. However, the complexity of DP-D (hybrid FEC + redundancy + retransmission + mode switching) and the safety criticality of the requirement warrant detailed decomposition of sub-FRs to ensure each mechanism is properly designed, integrated, and validated. Recommend proceeding to next level of functional decomposition for each sub-FR.
decision.justification_detail: The refined FR-NGHE-009 is decomposed into four sub-FRs that address distinct aspects of packet loss mitigation: (1) detection and reporting, (2) error correction/retransmission, (3) adaptation, and (4) safety monitoring. Each sub-FR should be decomposed further to define specific DPs and IMs. For example, FR-NGHE-009.2 (error correction) could decompose into FEC codec design, retransmission buffer management, and timeout logic. FR-NGHE-009.4 (safety monitoring) could decompose into threshold monitoring, safe-state transition logic, and operator notification. Detailed decomposition will ensure that DP-D is fully realized and validated.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation subsystem shall implement packet loss detection and reporting.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation subsystem shall employ error correction or retransmission mechanisms to minimize packet loss impact on command/feedback integrity.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation subsystem shall adapt communication parameters (e.g., compression, redundancy) based on network conditions to maintain packet loss within acceptable bounds.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The safety services subsystem shall monitor teleoperation communication quality and trigger safe-state transitions if packet loss exceeds threshold.

assumptions: Customer-agreed network conditions are well-defined and stable (e.g., 4G LTE, WiFi 6, or private network with specified latency/jitter bounds).
Redundant communication channels are available (e.g., dual cellular, WiFi + LTE, or dual fiber).
FEC overhead (bandwidth and latency) is acceptable for teleoperation use case.
Safety Services can reliably detect and respond to packet loss threshold violations with deterministic latency.
Operator tolerance for latency and mode switching is acceptable (to be validated with stakeholders).
Network SLA can be negotiated and enforced with network provider or site infrastructure team.
open_risks: Network conditions may be vague or highly variable, making ≤1% packet loss target unachievable. Mitigation: Establish clear network SLA with customer.
Redundant communication channels may not be available in all deployment scenarios. Mitigation: Confirm network topology with customer and site infrastructure team.
FEC overhead may increase latency beyond operator tolerance. Mitigation: Prototype and measure latency impact; tune FEC parameters.
Safety Services may be slow to detect and respond to packet loss threshold violations. Mitigation: Define deterministic monitoring latency budget and test integration.
Mode switching may introduce operator confusion or safety issues. Mitigation: Ensure clear communication of mode changes and test operator acceptance.
DP-D complexity may introduce bugs or integration issues. Mitigation: Rigorous design review, unit testing, integration testing, and field validation.
evidence_needed: Network SLA document specifying dual channels, bandwidth, latency, jitter bounds, and availability targets.
Customer acceptance criteria for packet loss (≤1% or alternative target) and agreed network conditions.
Detailed DP-D specification: FEC scheme, redundancy strategy, retransmission parameters, mode switching thresholds, and safe-state transition logic.
SafetyAlert interface contract: packet loss metrics, threshold definitions, safe-state transition logic, and latency budget.
Teleoperation sub-component design: FEC codec, redundancy controller, retransmission buffer, quality monitor, and internal interfaces.
Testing plan: unit tests, integration tests, testbed validation, and field trials.
Operator acceptance testing: latency tolerance, mode switching clarity, and situational awareness impact.
Safety validation: safe-state transition logic, threshold monitoring, and failure modes analysis.
